Geodesic Pathwise Polynomial Annihilator Experiments

  • Purpose
  • Disk Size
  • Selected Radius Search Size
  • Composite-Geodesic Availability
  • Geodesic Skeleton Coverage
  • Polynomial-Residual Row Thinning

  • Polynomial Residual Ratios
  • Intrinsic Versus Ambient Structure On Quadrics
  • Results Summary And Discussion
  • Appendix
    • Run Metadata
    • Run Summary Table
    • Construction Summary Table
    • Nullity Table
    • Polynomial Score Table
    • Local Dimension Diagnostic Table
    • Local Dimension Vote Table
    • Reproducibility Commands

Purpose

This report evaluates an experimental graph operator intended to annihilate intrinsic low-degree polynomials by sampling geodesic paths in local graph disks. For a weighted graph, the disk around vertex \(v\) is

\[ B_G(v,\varepsilon)=\{u\in V : d_G(u,v)\le \varepsilon\}, \]

where \(d_G\) is graph-geodesic distance. The implementation samples composite geodesic paths through \(v\), constructs one-dimensional polynomial annihilator rows along those paths, and stacks the rows into a sparse matrix \(A\). If the construction behaves as an intrinsic polynomial annihilator on a sampled surface, then sampled intrinsic polynomials \(p\) of degree at most \(d\) should satisfy approximately

\[ A p \approx 0. \]

The experiments ask two questions. First, on sampled \([0,1]^2\), does the estimated nullity of \(A\) stabilize near the dimensions

\[ \dim \mathcal P_{\le d}(\mathbb R^2)=\binom{d+2}{2}=3,6,10 \quad\text{for}\quad d=1,2,3? \]

Second, on sampled quadrics, does the operator respond to intrinsic polynomial structure in the surface parameters rather than merely to ambient coordinate polynomials in \((X,Y,Z)\)?

The graph families tested here are the Delaunay 1-skeleton, symmetric kNN, radius graphs, and cKNN graphs. Two radius rules are compared. The fixed rule uses the graph distance to the \(k_{\mathrm{disk}}\)-th graph-geodesic neighbor. The adaptive rule increases \(k_{\mathrm{disk}}\) until the number of available composite geodesics reaches a specified minimum, or until a maximum search size is reached. Detailed run tables and reproducibility code are collected in the appendix so the main text can focus on the figures.

Disk Size

The disk radius controls what local geometry the operator sees. If disks are too small, there may be too few usable geodesic paths to identify multivariate polynomial structure. If disks are too large, the operator may mix geometry from regions where a local polynomial approximation is no longer appropriate. The first diagnostic therefore measures the distribution of

\[ |B_G(v,\varepsilon_v)| \]

across vertices. Under the adaptive rule, \(\varepsilon_v\) is selected as

\[ \varepsilon_v =\min \{\varepsilon_{v,k}: |\Gamma(v,\varepsilon_{v,k})|\ge m_{\min}\}, \]

subject to the maximum allowed search size.

The adaptive rule often selects larger local disks than the fixed rule. This is expected: it is no longer trying to equalize disk size, but instead to guarantee a minimum supply of usable composite geodesics. The figure also shows that the extra disk growth is graph-dependent, which means the selected radius itself is an important diagnostic of local graph geometry.

Selected Radius Search Size

The adaptive rule changes the radius by increasing the neighbor index \(k_{\mathrm{disk}}\). The selected value is

\[ k_v^\star=\min\{k: |\Gamma(v,\varepsilon_{v,k})|\ge m_{\min}\}. \]

If \(k_v^\star\) is frequently near the maximum allowed value, the graph does not provide enough local composite geodesics at the tested scale.

The selected-\(k_{\mathrm{disk}}\) distributions show how much each graph family must expand its local disk to obtain enough composite geodesics. A concentration at small values means the local graph already supplies adequate path geometry. A heavy right tail means the graph requires larger neighborhoods before the operator can even be assembled with the requested path availability.

Composite-Geodesic Availability

The operator is built from pathwise annihilator rows. The relevant local sample size is therefore not just the disk size but the number of composite geodesics available through each center vertex. For each center \(v\), we count candidate paths \(\gamma\in\Gamma(v,\varepsilon_v)\) that pass through \(v\) and satisfy the composite-geodesic test. This diagnostic measures

\[ |\Gamma(v,\varepsilon_v)|. \]

This figure is the main check on whether the adaptive radius rule succeeded. Compared with the fixed rule, the adaptive rule shifts the composite-geodesic count distribution away from zero and toward the requested minimum. Remaining low-count centers identify vertices where even the maximum allowed search size does not produce enough local path geometry.

Geodesic Skeleton Coverage

The skeleton row family asks a different question from the local composite operator. Instead of constructing a disk at every vertex, it first selects a small set of long graph-geodesic paths. The first path is a graph diameter. Subsequent passes attach vertices that are poorly covered by the current skeleton. In the cross-enriched variant, additional local connector geodesics are added between nearby skeleton paths. The diagnostic below measures the coverage multiplicity

\[ c(v)=\#\{\gamma_s: v\in\gamma_s\} \]

and the distance from each vertex to the selected skeleton. A good first skeleton should cover most vertices at least once; a useful enriched skeleton should raise coverage in regions that need transverse constraints.

The skeleton family is intentionally sparse. Its first success criterion is not matching the local composite operator, but obtaining deterministic path coverage that can support ordinary one-dimensional sliding-window trend-filter rows. If cross-enrichment improves coverage without making the null space collapse, it is a promising row-generation strategy for the next round of operator tests.

Polynomial-Residual Row Thinning

The unthinned operator keeps every pathwise annihilator row that passes the local geodesic and mirror-extension rules. The experimental thinning rule first builds a local metric-MDS polynomial probe matrix \(P_v\) on each graph disk and then greedily keeps rows with small

\[ \frac{\|aP_v\|_2}{\|P_v\|_F} \]

while preserving row rank and vertex coverage. This diagnostic asks how aggressively the rule reduces the local row stack before we inspect nullity and polynomial residuals.

The smoke and full grids now use deterministic thinning: centers are processed in increasing graph-degree order, candidate path caps are resolved by stable path-length and canonical-path ordering, and residual-score ties are broken by path length and canonical path label. This makes repeated runs comparable without depending on random path subsampling.

If this distribution is near one, thinning is behaving conservatively. If it is near zero, the local polynomial probe is rejecting most path rows and the operator may become underconstrained. The useful regime should reduce redundant rows while preserving enough coverage for nullity and residual tests.

Local Coordinate Dimension Diagnostics

The pathwise operator is defined from geodesic paths, but the implementation roadmap also asks whether each disk looks locally one-, two-, or three-dimensional after metric embedding. For a local disk \(B_G(v,\varepsilon_v)\), we embed the induced weighted graph in dimensions \(q=1,\ldots,q_{\max}\) and compute the relative edge-length error

\[ E_q(v) = \left( \frac{\sum_{(i,j)\in E_v}(\|z_i-z_j\|-\ell_{ij})^2} {\sum_{(i,j)\in E_v}\ell_{ij}^2} \right)^{1/2}. \]

The fast diagnostic backend uses local graph-geodesic cmdscale; selected smoke runs additionally exercise the edge-KK diagnostic path. For dimensions 2 and 3, that path can use weighted GRIP as an initializer. For dimensions above 3, it uses metric MDS followed by edge-KK when the installed grip namespace exposes the newer high-dimensional edge-KK functions. The selected local dimension is a stress-elbow diagnostic, not a theorem about the null space.

This diagnostic separates a geometric question from the annihilator row-stack question. If stress drops sharply from dimension one to two and then stabilizes, the local disk behaves like a sampled surface patch. If stress remains large in dimension three, the current local coordinate engine is at its useful cap and the downstream thinning rules should treat the disk cautiously.

The stress elbow is only one estimator. The diagnostic table also records a metric-MDS spectrum elbow and a graph-Laplacian Weyl-slope estimate. Their agreement or disagreement is useful because local graph disks can be irregular: one estimator may detect metric embedding dimension, while another detects diffusive graph dimension.

Nullity At A Fixed Numerical Tolerance

The formal null-space target for a degree-\(d\) intrinsic polynomial annihilator on a two-dimensional domain is

\[ \dim \ker(A)\approx \binom{d+2}{2}. \]

The next figure compares the estimated nullity at relative singular-value threshold \(10^{-6}\) with the target values \(3,6,10\). The dashed line marks perfect agreement.

The fixed-threshold nullity still does not stabilize near \(3,6,10\). The adaptive rule improves the most obvious underconstrained radius-graph failures for lower orders by forcing more composite geodesics into the operator, but it does not create the desired polynomial null space. Delaunay, cKNN, and symmetric kNN configurations remain mostly overconstrained, with estimated nullity near one. This answers the first nullity question in the negative for the present construction: path availability is necessary, but not sufficient, for recovering the expected intrinsic polynomial null-space dimension.

Nullity Sensitivity

A single numerical tolerance can hide whether the singular spectrum contains a near-null plateau. We therefore also compute

\[ \widehat{\dim\ker(A;\tau)} = n - \#\{s_i(A): s_i(A)>\tau s_1(A)\} \]

for several relative tolerances \(\tau\). A stable intrinsic null space would appear as a plateau near \(3,6,10\) across a nontrivial tolerance range.

The sensitivity plot reinforces the fixed-tolerance result. There is no broad, stable plateau at the target dimensions. The adaptive rule reduces some large-nullity artifacts by adding rows where the fixed rule had too few paths, but the resulting spectrum still does not organize itself around the intrinsic dimensions \(3,6,10\). This indicates that the current pathwise row stack is better viewed as a residual diagnostic than as a calibrated null-space construction.

Polynomial Residual Ratios

Because exact numerical nullity is brittle on finite sampled graphs, we also evaluate sampled polynomial residuals directly. For a design matrix \(P\), define

\[ R(P)=\frac{\|AP\|_F}{\|P\|_F}. \]

For a degree-\(d\) annihilator, residuals for intrinsic monomials of degree at most \(d\) should be smaller than residuals for exact degree \(d+1\) controls.

The residual-ratio experiment is more encouraging than the nullity experiment. The adaptive rule generally lowers intrinsic residuals by supplying more path constraints, and intrinsic degree-\(d\) residuals are usually smaller than degree-\(d+1\) controls. This means the pathwise rows do encode useful local polynomial information even though the singular spectrum does not yet produce the desired nullity. Radius graphs remain the most delicate case: adaptive expansion helps, but high-order radius-graph operators can still be underconstrained when the maximum disk size is reached before enough usable paths are found.

Intrinsic Versus Ambient Structure On Quadrics

On a sampled quadric, the coordinates \((u,v)\) are intrinsic parameters, while \((X,Y,Z)\) are ambient coordinates. The intended operator should respond to polynomial behavior in intrinsic coordinates. For example, on a paraboloid, \(Z\) is quadratic in \((u,v)\), so it should be treated differently by degree-1 and degree-2 annihilators. The following figure compares residuals for intrinsic and ambient function families.

The quadric diagnostics suggest partial intrinsic behavior, but not yet a clean intrinsic polynomial annihilator. Intrinsic targets usually have lower residuals than degree-\(d+1\) controls, and degree-2 operators improve on quadratic surface-coordinate behavior relative to degree-1 operators. However, the ambient and intrinsic families are not cleanly separated across all graphs. This is expected for a first pathwise construction because ambient coordinates and intrinsic parameters are strongly correlated on the sampled surfaces, especially at this sample size.

Results Summary And Discussion

The experiments answer the main questions in a deliberately conservative way. The estimated nullity does not yet stabilize near \(3,6,10\) for degrees \(1,2,3\). The adaptive radius rule fixes one important construction problem: the median number of composite geodesics increases to the requested target for Delaunay, cKNN, and symmetric kNN graphs, and improves substantially for radius graphs. However, the expected polynomial nullity still does not emerge. This separates two issues that were previously entangled. The old fixed-radius rule could fail simply because too few paths were available; the new adaptive rule shows that even with adequate path availability, the current row stack is usually too constraining.

The residual diagnostics are more promising. Even though the operator does not produce the target null-space dimension, it often yields smaller residuals on intrinsic degree-\(d\) polynomial families than on degree-\(d+1\) controls. The adaptive rule usually improves these residuals, which supports the decision to control path availability directly. The remaining gap is therefore not merely a radius-selection problem; it is a row-selection or local-coordinate problem.

The quadric experiments also support a cautious interpretation. The operator shows some sensitivity to intrinsic polynomial structure in the parameter domain, but it does not yet sharply separate intrinsic polynomial behavior from ambient coordinate polynomial behavior. At the present sample sizes and graph densities, this is not surprising: the ambient coordinates are smooth functions of the intrinsic coordinates, and the current operator is built from one-dimensional path restrictions rather than from an explicit local tangent-coordinate model.

The graph comparison is informative. Delaunay 1-skeleton graphs are a useful geometric baseline because they maintain planar local structure and avoid some of the high-branching behavior of kNN graphs. Symmetric kNN and cKNN graphs give more composite paths, but they can also impose many overlapping constraints. Radius graphs are the least stable in this experiment because comparable disk sizes do not guarantee enough composite geodesics.

The immediate methodological conclusion is that the next version should not aim only to add more paths. The adaptive rule should remain part of the construction because it prevents path-starved local operators, but it must be paired with a path-selection or local-basis step whose goal is to preserve the expected polynomial null space while maintaining adequate coverage. The new local coordinate diagnostics make this more concrete: before thinning rows, we can ask whether each disk has a stable low-dimensional metric realization and whether the selected local dimension is consistent with the polynomial probe space being tested. The most useful next experiments are local row thinning by rank contribution, tangent-coordinate regression annihilators inside graph disks, and explicit comparison of row normalization and vertex normalization rules. The current report establishes the diagnostic framework needed to test those choices.

Construction Summary Table

aggregate(
  cbind(n.disk.vertices, n.composite.available, n.paths.used,
        selected.disk.k, candidate.operator.rows, selected.operator.rows,
        n.operator.rows, used.mirror.proxy) ~
    dataset + graph.type + degree + path.family + disk.radius.rule + disk.k + row.selection +
    center.order + candidate.cap.mode,
  data = vertex.table,
  FUN = function(z) if (is.logical(z)) mean(z) else stats::median(z)
)
##       dataset         graph.type degree               path.family
## 1  paraboloid delaunay.1skeleton      1 local.composite.geodesics

Reproducibility Commands

# Smoke run
GFLOW_GEO_ANNIHILATOR_MODE=smoke \
  Rscript dev/graph-trend-filtering/render_geodesic_annihilator_experiments.R

# Full run
GFLOW_GEO_ANNIHILATOR_MODE=full \
  Rscript dev/graph-trend-filtering/render_geodesic_annihilator_experiments.R
